one_line_takeaway: Prompt injection has empirically evolved into a multi-stage 'promptware' kill chain (initial access, privilege escalation, reconnaissance, persistence, C2, lateral movement, actions on objective) -- across 36 documented incidents from 2023-2026, kill-chain coverage rose from 2-3 stages to routinely 4-5 stages, with only 2 incidents reaching promptware-native C2 and none yet reaching all seven stages, arguing the field needs defense-in-depth rather than prompt-injection-only mitigations.
key_finding: Of 36 documented incidents analyzed, at least 21 (specifically noted as the 2025-2026 subset) traverse four or more of the seven kill-chain stages, and the authors state at least 15 documented attacks overall reach four-plus stages (contributions section) / fifteen of the twenty-one 2025-2026 incidents reach four-plus stages with six reaching five stages (Section IV analysis). Table III gives the full stage-count distribution: 2023 (n=3): 1 two-stage, 2 three-stage, 0 four-stage, 0 five-stage; 2024 (n=12): 1 two-stage, 4 three-stage, 4 four-stage, 3 five-stage; 2025-2026 (n=21): 1 two-stage, 5 three-stage, 9 four-stage, 6 five-stage; totals across all 36: 3 two-stage, 11 three-stage, 13 four-stage, 9 five-stage, 0 six- or seven-stage. No incident in the dataset has achieved all seven stages; the maximum observed coverage is five stages. Only two incidents (ChatGPT ZombAI, Oct 2024, and the Reprompt attack against Microsoft Copilot, Jan 2026) achieved promptware-native command-and-control, as opposed to falling back to conventional binary-level C2 after achieving RCE (e.g., ZombAIs, Devin AI RCE). AI worms show the highest per-category kill-chain coverage (2 of 3 documented worm incidents reached five stages). Persistence appeared in 6 of 12 2024 incidents and 12 of 21 2025-2026 incidents. Lateral movement grew from 0 of 3 (2023) to 3 of 12 (2024) to 8 of 21 (2025-2026).
core_contribution: The paper (Brodt, Feldman, Schneier, Nassi) argues that prompt injection has evolved from a single-shot input-manipulation vulnerability into a multistep malware delivery mechanism the authors name 'promptware.' Its own contributions are: (1) a Table I comparison of prompt injection against SQL injection, script injection, and OS command injection across ten dimensions (language, modality, target layer, root cause, executor, outcome determinism, runtime, compromised space, blast radius, outcomes), used to argue that the 'prompt injection = SQL injection' analogy understates severity; (2) a novel seven-stage 'promptware kill chain' taxonomy -- Initial Access (prompt injection), Privilege Escalation (jailbreaking), Reconnaissance, Persistence (retrieval-dependent vs. retrieval-independent), Command & Control, Lateral Movement (on-device: cross-agent/cross-application; off-device: cross-client/cross-application), and Actions on Objective; (3) a curated analysis of 36 documented incidents/studies from Feb 2023 to Jan 2026 (Table II), each hand-coded against the seven stages, showing kill-chain coverage growing from 2-3 stages in 2023 to routinely 4-5 stages in 2025-2026; and (4) a defense-in-depth mitigation review (Section V, Table IV) that scores each mitigation class (e.g., prompt injection sanitizers, plan-then-execute, instruction-data separation, alignment, prompt perturbation, ensemble oversight) along Type (Prevention/Mitigation/Remediation), which kill-chain stage(s) it addresses, deployment layer (Model/I-O-wrapper/Architecture/External), and qualitative usability/deployment/maintenance cost.
threat_model: The paper's threat model is an adversary who injects instructions into an LLM application's context window (directly, as the user, or indirectly, via compromised external content such as webpages, emails, calendar invites, GitHub issues, or documents) and then leverages the application's own permissions, tools, memory/retrieval systems, and connectivity to other agents/applications/users to escalate from a one-shot exploit into a persistent, remotely controllable, laterally propagating compromise. It explicitly excludes traditional software exploits (e.g., memory-corruption buffer overflows in LLM-enabled apps) that are not initiated by a prompt and do not exploit the LLM itself, even if the ultimate outcome is similar. The paper is explicitly framed as taxonomic/analytical rather than as a new attack or defense technique -- it does not itself execute any attack; it re-analyzes 36 already-published incidents and studies.
limitations: The paper does not state these as limitations explicitly in the excerpted sections, but grounded observations: the incident dataset (n=36) is a curated, non-systematic selection of 'prominent' incidents/studies chosen by the authors, not a comprehensive or randomly sampled corpus, so stage-coverage trends could reflect selection/reporting bias (more sophisticated attacks are more likely to be publicized) rather than a representative shift in the overall attack population. Stage classification is manual/qualitative and the authors themselves acknowledge stage boundaries 'are not always sharp' and that attackers may skip, merge, or reorder stages -- i.e., the taxonomy is offered as an analytical tool, not a rigid or independently validated coding scheme, and no inter-rater reliability or coding methodology is reported. The mitigation effectiveness ratings in Table IV are qualitative judgments (circle-fill notation) rather than measured outcomes from the authors' own experiments. The work is descriptive/taxonomic and proposes no new detection or defense mechanism of its own; it synthesizes existing mitigations rather than testing them.

datasets_used: Self-compiled corpus of 36 documented promptware incidents and research demonstrations spanning February 2023 to January 2026, tabulated in Table II with per-incident stage coding
quantitative_results: 36 total incidents analyzed; stage distribution (Table III): 2023 n=3 (1 two-stage, 2 three-stage); 2024 n=12 (1 two-stage, 4 three-stage, 4 four-stage, 3 five-stage); 2025-2026 n=21 (1 two-stage, 5 three-stage, 9 four-stage, 6 five-stage); totals 3 two-stage, 11 three-stage, 13 four-stage, 9 five-stage, 0 six/seven-stage
At least 15 of the 21 documented 2025-2026 incidents demonstrated four or more kill-chain stages; 6 of 21 reached five stages
Number of attacks achieving four-or-more stages rose from 0 (2023) to 7 (2024) to 15 (2025-2026)
Only 2 of 36 incidents achieved promptware-native C2: ChatGPT ZombAI (Oct 2024) and the Reprompt attack against Microsoft Copilot (Jan 2026)
Persistence observed in 6 of 12 (2024) and 12 of 21 (2025-2026) incidents
Lateral movement observed in 0 of 3 (2023), 3 of 12 (2024), 8 of 21 (2025-2026) incidents
7 of 21 (2025-2026) incidents targeted AI coding assistants specifically
2 of 3 AI-worm incidents in the dataset reached five kill-chain stages, the highest per-category coverage
No incident in the dataset reached all seven kill-chain stages; maximum observed was five stages
Freysa AI heist (Nov 2024) cited as extracting $47,000 from an AI-controlled cryptocurrency wallet via social engineering (role-playing as administrator)
ForcedLeak attack against Salesforce Agentforce cited as exploiting a $5 expired whitelisted domain purchase to exfiltrate CRM data
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines; the paper instead structurally compares prompt injection/promptware against three other injection-attack classes (SQL injection, script injection such as JS/Python, and OS command injection) across ten qualitative dimensions in Table I
